metadata.name: Julio Simmons
metadata.protocol: 4-direction expert panel verification (Demographer, Behavioral Economist, Political Scientist, Psychologist)

direction_1_seed_vs_interview.description: Seed data compared against interview transcript for consistency, enrichment, and contradiction.

demographer.detail: Seed states age 49, male, divorced, Rochester NY 14618, graduate education, not_in_workforce. Interview confirms all identically: age 49 (Q85: DOB October 14, 1976), male (Q89), divorced ~6 years ago (Q84), Brighton/Rochester 14618 (Q08), master's degree (Q90), not currently employed (Q16).

Seed states 'German-Irish descent' and 'middle-class family' in Rochester. Interview enriches with specific detail: dad Karl worked in Kodak-adjacent tooling/die manufacturing, mom Eileen was medical records clerk at Strong Memorial Hospital for 20+ years, grandparents on dad's side made sauerkraut, mom's side had big Irish Christmas gatherings (Q01, Q05, Q100-Q107).

Seed mentions RIT and creative coding but no specifics. Interview specifies: B.S. in Applied Mathematics from RIT, M.S. in Computational Science from University of Rochester, attended Brighton High School (Q01, Q90).

Seed provides no family details beyond marital status. Interview identifies full family: parents Karl and Eileen (mid-70s, Brighton), younger sister Greta (44, nurse practitioner, Syracuse, married to Paul, children Milo 12 and Sophie 9), ex-wife Diana (social worker), Uncle Frank (Buffalo, retired electrician), cousin Heinrich (vineyard near Seneca Lake) (Q05, Q06).
Seed states bachelors_field as 'stem.' Interview confirms: applied mathematics at RIT, computational science at U of R (Q01, Q90). Both STEM fields.

Seed states occupation as 'not_in_workforce.' Interview provides trajectory: left workforce ~3 years ago after burnout cycles, previously freelanced data visualization, adjuncted at RIT, worked at analytics firm. Currently draws down savings supplemented by sporadic freelance at $500-800/month (Q01, Q16, Q65).

behavioral_economist.detail: Seed describes 'competitive drive pushes them to secure grant funding.' Interview reveals the grant pursuit is real but marked by devastating failure: 42-page application, 3 months of work, form rejection with zero feedback. The competitive framing from the seed is tempered by the interview's revelation of how grant rejection produces identity-level crisis (Q04, Q24 follow-up, Q44).

Seed mentions 'leveraging Python, MATLAB, and creative coding to design immersive workshops.' Interview quantifies the economic reality: workshops bring $100-120/session at Rochester Brainery, freelance gigs pay $400-800/project, total monthly earned income $500-800 in good months, zero in bad months (Q16, Q71, Q72).

Seed mentions career goals of launching a makerspace and securing grants. Interview provides granular financial picture: business plan 70% complete, $30K-50K estimated startup cost, targeting Monroe County community development grant with June deadline, pop-up workshops planned for summer demand validation (Q108 follow-up).

Seed persona references 'budget constraints keep most trips regional.' Interview quantifies: monthly fixed costs ~$1,700, income ~$500-800/month, drawing down savings at several hundred dollars/month, emergency fund covers 3-4 months of expenses, $8,000 out-of-pocket insurance maximum equals entire emergency fund (Q56, Q57, Q40, Q82).

Seed culinary_persona describes experimental cooking with specialty ingredients (miso-glazed black cod, sous-vide duck breast, Sichuan pepper chocolate truffles, heirloom tomatoes from farmers' market). Interview confirms sourcing from Rochester Public Market and farmers' market weekly, but adds budget-constrained fallback: rice, beans, pasta, Aldi vegetables during lean months (Q15, Q18, Q56, Q81).

political_scientist.detail: Seed contains no explicit political information beyond 'progressive Midwestern values' in cultural_background. Interview provides extensive political detail: votes in every election including school board, leans strongly Democratic, issue-driven (education, environment, healthcare, arts funding), donates to local candidates and Rochester Environmental Action League, has done phone banking (Q23-Q30, Q95-Q96).

Seed's 'progressive Midwestern values that emphasize education, self-reliance, and community participation' is confirmed and elaborated. Interview shows these translate into specific policy positions: strong social safety net, aggressive climate action, institutional skepticism tempered by data trust, equity-focused community building (Q24, Q25).

Seed mentions 'social equity and environmental stewardship.' Interview provides concrete actions: pushed STEM outreach into underserved neighborhoods (Beechwood, Northeast), offered scholarship workshop spots, reduced meat consumption for environmental reasons, bikes when possible, buys local (Q24, Q26, Q29).

Seed mentions no civic engagement specifics. Interview reveals: League of Women Voters guide user, creates candidate research spreadsheets with weighted issue scores, cross-references Democrat and Chronicle and Rochester Beacon coverage, emails candidates directly for small races (Q23 follow-ups).

psychologist.detail: Seed persona describes 'anxiety and a competitive streak often drive them between bursts of creative brilliance and bouts of self-critical burnout.' Interview provides the full behavioral cycle: idea triggers intense focus (10-12 hour sessions, skipping meals, chest tightness), followed by 2-3 days of marathon work, then 12-14 hour sleep crashes with feelings of hollowness and 'why did I bother' (Q01 follow-up). This is richly detailed beyond seed's summary.

Seed mentions 'mindfulness meditation to manage his anxiety.' Interview describes a comprehensive coping scaffold: 20-minute daily meditation (Insight Timer, body-scan preferred), mood/sleep journal (1-10 rating), full-spectrum light therapy lamp (30 min/morning Oct-Apr), acupuncture for jaw tension, low-dose prescription for sleep/racing thoughts, social commitments treated as non-negotiable behavioral activation (Q31, Q37, Q39, Q45, Q50).

Seed describes 'restless polymath' who is 'interested in everything but never seemed to settle.' Interview reveals this is both identity-affirming (the lab experience with Dr. Tanaka: 'your weird combination of skills isn't weird -- it's exactly what we need') and self-sabotaging (portfolio website redesign loop, constant project-jumping that strained marriage) (Q01, Q02 follow-up, Q16 follow-up).

Seed mentions divorce with no behavioral detail. Interview reveals post-divorce avoidant relational pattern: replays conversations, catalogues dating missteps obsessively, paces apartment, drafts and deletes texts 4-5 times, lets follow-up window close. Friend Marcus has identified the pattern. Physical symptoms accompany rumination: knotted stomach, inability to settle (Q05, Q05 follow-up).

Seed sports_persona mentions desire to 'improve lap times' and a 'competitive edge.' Interview acknowledges the competitive streak but presents it as subordinate to the collaborative/community orientation. The seed's competitive framing is somewhat overstated relative to interview self-presentation, which is more self-deprecating and uncertainty-driven (Q07 follow-up, Q109).

Seed describes the persona broadly but interview reveals a three-week functional shutdown (Jan-Feb 2026): sleeping until 9-10am, catastrophic thoughts ('nobody would notice if I disappeared'), eating one meal/day (toast or cereal), unable to engage with work, 8-pound weight loss. Sister Greta intervened, stocked fridge, sat with him. This severity of behavioral disruption is not captured in the seed (Q33, Q33 follow-ups).

direction_2_interview_vs_extracted_profi.description: Interview transcript compared against extracted_profile for accuracy, omissions, and misrepresentations.

demographer.detail: Profile header demographics (age 49, male, divorced, Rochester NY 14618, master's degree, not in workforce) match interview exactly (Q84-Q90, Q08, Q16, Q65).
Profile does not record date of birth (October 14, 1976) from Q85. Should be included in demographics header.
Profile does not record race/ethnicity explicitly in header. Interview states 'White. German-Irish background' (Q89). Should be in demographics.
Profile does not record birthplace (Rochester, New York, Q87) or U.S.-born status (Q86) in header.

Profile demographer summary accurately captures the downward mobility narrative: graduate-educated but not in workforce, drawing down savings, income well below poverty threshold relative to credentials. Matches Q16, Q56, Q61, Q83.

Profile demographer summary mentions parents' ages (mid-70s) but does not record their education: dad has trade certificate in tool and die making (Q100), mom has associate's degree in health information management from Monroe Community College (Q101). Should be included.

behavioral_economist.detail: Profile behavioral economist summary accurately captures present-bias (choosing passion over pay), identity-based loss aversion, risk-tolerant on income/risk-averse on debt, mental accounting, and consumption-mood feedback loops. All match interview evidence.

Profile does not capture the deliberate no-credit-card commitment device. Interview explicitly states this is intentional self-regulation: 'I know myself well enough to know that easy access to credit would be a problem for me' (Q60). This is a significant behavioral-economic finding.
Profile does not capture the bartering behavior within the maker community (built a website in exchange for woodworking, Q60). This demonstrates alternative exchange norms and community reciprocity.
Profile does not capture the car payment ($270/month, 8 months remaining) from Q63 as a significant budget item and near-term financial relief opportunity.

Profile does not record the tax filing behavior: TurboTax Self-Employed, tracks 1099 income and deductions (home office, art supplies, software, mileage), makes estimated quarterly payments, directs ~$300 refund to savings (Q75). This demonstrates sophisticated self-employed financial management.

Profile accurately identifies the financial countdown clock anxiety and the monthly savings-check ritual. Interview confirms: checks balance once monthly during budget spreadsheet session, 'ruins the next hour,' previously checked compulsively before establishing monthly cadence (Q61 follow-up).

political_scientist.detail: Profile political scientist summary accurately captures consistent voting, Democratic lean, issue-driven orientation, moderate institutional skepticism, and shift from partisan activism toward localized community building. Matches Q23-Q30, Q95-Q96.

Profile community_and_civic section does not list Julio's specific priority policy issues: education funding, Great Lakes/Finger Lakes water quality, healthcare access for non-traditionally-employed, NEA/state arts council budgets (Q23 follow-up). These are his primary political motivators.

Profile does not capture Julio's self-identified evolution from ardent partisan (volunteered for campaigns, yard signs in 20s-30s) to 'critical ally' focused on local competence over party (Q95 follow-up). This is a significant political trajectory.

Profile does not capture the intergenerational political negotiation with father Karl: dad more conservative on fiscal issues, Julio uses specific human stories rather than data/abstract arguments to bridge the gap, improv 'yes and' technique applied to political disagreements (Q25 follow-up, Q30 follow-up).

Profile accurately captures concrete racial equity actions: STEM outreach pop-ups in Beechwood/Northeast neighborhoods, workshop scholarships, adjusting volunteer approach to be less instructional and more listening-centered (Q26 follow-up, Q29).

psychologist.detail: Profile psychologist summary accurately captures high Openness, variable Conscientiousness, moderate Extraversion with crowd sensitivity, competitive self-evaluation, and low Emotional Stability with somatic manifestations. Matches Q01, Q05, Q31, Q43, Q45.

Profile does not capture the full-body presentation shutdown during graduate thesis colloquium: vision narrowing, inability to speak, hand trembling, sat on bathroom floor for 20 minutes (Q45 follow-up). This was the pivotal moment of self-recognition that physical stress responses were beyond normal nervousness.

Profile does not capture the selective disclosure pattern: Marcus knows most, Greta understands from clinical standpoint, Priya recognizes in-the-moment signs and provides breathing exercises, parents lack framework entirely -- mom says 'relax more,' dad thinks willpower should suffice (Q45 follow-up). This differential vulnerability map is psychologically significant.

Profile does not capture the post-divorce relational avoidance cycle: good date triggers exhaustive post-mortem replay, physical pacing, stomach knots, drafts-and-deletes texts 4-5 times, window for natural follow-up closes, pattern repeats. Marcus has named this pattern explicitly (Q05 follow-ups).

Profile does not capture the family emotional communication deficit: 'love but not language for feeling,' mother proxies emotional information ('your father had a hard week'), Julio attempts to model honesty with Greta as pattern-breaking, wishes father could say 'I'm scared about getting old' (Q46 follow-up). This family system dynamic shapes his own emotional processing.

Profile correctly identifies the creativity-as-vulnerability paradox: flow states are both greatest resource and greatest risk, triggering unsustainable intensity cycles. Interview confirms with specific example: 8-hour coding session skipping meals, jaw clenched all day, worked until 3am, followed by next-day crash on couch feeling depleted (Q15 follow-up).

direction_3_profile_internal_consistency.description: Extracted profile checked for internal contradictions, logical inconsistencies, and cross-section coherence.

demographer.detail: All demographic fields in the header are internally consistent with the body of the profile. Age 49, Rochester NY 14618, divorced, master's degree, not in workforce are referenced consistently across all 15 preference categories, 4 summaries, and 6 others_misc subcategories.

Family details referenced across sections are consistent: parents Karl and Eileen (Brighton, mid-70s) appear in social_and_relationships, cultural_and_linguistic, and life_events_and_transitions without contradiction.

Profile sports_and_fitness section states cycling group is 'April-October, Sunday mornings.' However, Q18 describes cycling starting back up and Q15 says the cycling group runs in summer. The profile's date range is reasonable but the day is inconsistent: Q18 says 'Sunday mornings' for cycling but Q15's weekly schedule puts cycling on 'Saturdays' for physical activity. Seed sports_persona does not specify a day. Need to reconcile -- Q18 explicitly says 'Sunday mornings on the Erie Canal towpath,' so the profile should use Sunday.

behavioral_economist.detail: Financial figures across sections are internally consistent: rent $950, health insurance $450, car payment $270, groceries $300-350, utilities $100-150, prescription $30, cat supplies ~$40, acupuncture $60 occasional = ~$2,200 typical month against $500-800/month income + savings draw. The profile's financial picture is internally coherent.

The behavioral patterns are internally coherent across sections: spreadsheet tracking appears in finances (budget), politics (candidate research), health (mood journal), and work (project management). This systematic data-tracking behavior is consistently represented as a core trait.

Profile food_and_dining section lists 'Genny Cream Ale at D&D nights' but does not mention Bloody Mary at brunch (Q38). The profile's music section mentions Spotify subscription paused during financial crunch. Home_and_lifestyle mentions 'pauses subscriptions when tight' which is consistent. No contradiction but the food_and_dining evidence citations are slightly incomplete -- Q38 provides significant drinking-behavior detail.

political_scientist.detail: Profile political scientist summary's characterization as 'issue-driven rather than partisan' is consistent with community_and_civic section's emphasis on local action and the behavioral economist's description of institutional skepticism. No contradictions across sections.

The racial equity engagement described in community_and_civic (outreach pop-ups in underserved neighborhoods, scholarship spots) is consistent with the political scientist summary's characterization of 'concrete, if modest, steps.' No inflation or deflation of engagement level across sections.

psychologist.detail: The coping scaffold described across health_and_wellness (meditation, light therapy, acupuncture, prescription), social_and_relationships (commitments as 'non-negotiable'), and the psychologist summary (external commitments as behavioral activation) are mutually reinforcing and internally coherent.

Profile psychologist summary characterizes Agreeableness as 'low-to-moderate' citing 'competitive streak, critical self-evaluation, willingness to disagree.' However, the interview shows high prosocial behavior: workshop scholarships, STEM volunteering, adjusting to be less instructional with minority families, phone banking despite discomfort, bartering, mentoring students. Agreeableness is better characterized as 'moderate-to-high' with the self-critical pattern being an inward rather than interpersonal trait.

The relational avoidance pattern in social_and_relationships (Hinge dating, struggles with follow-through) is consistent with the psychologist summary's 'avoidant tendencies in romantic contexts' and the life_events section's divorce listing. Cross-section coherence holds.

direction_4_completeness.description: Profile checked for gaps where interview provided information that the extracted profile failed to capture.

demographer.detail: Date of birth (October 14, 1976, Q85) not in profile header.
Race/ethnicity (White, German-Irish, Q89) not in profile header.
Birthplace (Rochester, NY, Q87) and U.S.-born status (Q86) not in profile header.

Parents' education: father Karl -- high school + trade certificate in tool and die making (Q100); mother Eileen -- associate degree in health information management from Monroe Community College (Q101). Not recorded in profile.
Household members at age 16: both parents and sister Greta in Brighton; childhood dog Fritz (German Shepherd) (Q98, Q99). Not in profile.

behavioral_economist.detail: Car payment: $270/month with 8 months remaining on a used Subaru Outback purchased ~3 years ago (Q63). This is a significant budget item and imminent expense elimination. Not in profile financial details.
No-credit-card commitment device: deliberate choice to avoid credit access as self-regulation (Q60). Not captured in profile.
Bartering within maker community: exchanged website development for woodworking (Q60). Not in profile.
Art sales as occasional income: sold painting for $400 at Makers' Row holiday craft fair, sold couple of paintings for ~$300 (Q60, Q81). Not in work_and_career income sources.
Financial security threshold self-identified as $36,000/year plus employer health insurance (Q83 follow-up). This is a key behavioral anchor for decision-making. Not in profile.

political_scientist.detail: Specific priority policy issues: education funding, Great Lakes/Finger Lakes water quality, healthcare access for non-traditionally-employed, NEA/state arts council budgets (Q23 follow-up). Not listed in community_and_civic.

Political trajectory: ardent partisan in 20s-30s (campaign volunteering, yard signs) evolving to issue-driven pragmatist focused on local competence (Q95 follow-up). Not in political scientist summary.

Intergenerational political negotiation: father Karl more conservative on fiscal issues, Julio bridges gap using specific human stories rather than data or abstract arguments (Q25 follow-up, Q30 follow-up). Not captured.

Institutional trust hierarchy: trusts scientific institutions (CDC, peer-reviewed research, PubMed), skeptical of grant bureaucracies and party machinery, moderate trust in local journalism, skeptical of social media as information source (Q24 follow-up, Q42). Not explicitly mapped in profile.

psychologist.detail: Thesis colloquium presentation shutdown: first recognition that stress responses exceeded normal nervousness -- vision narrowing, inability to speak, trembling hands, 20 minutes on bathroom floor (Q45 follow-up). Not in profile.

Selective emotional disclosure map: Marcus (most), Greta (clinical framework), Priya (in-moment recognition), parents (no framework, mom says 'relax more,' dad believes in willpower) (Q45 follow-up). Not captured.

Post-date rumination cycle with specific physical markers: replays conversation, magnifies pauses, paces apartment, stomach knotted, drafts/deletes texts 4-5 times, uses meditation or Brian Eno to reset but by then follow-up window has closed (Q05 follow-up). Partially noted but behavioral specificity missing.

Family emotional communication deficit: 'love but not language for feeling,' mother proxies father's emotions, Julio models honesty with Greta as deliberate pattern-breaking (Q46 follow-up). Not in profile.

Three core values self-identified in order: creation ('the core of who I am'), connection (fears losing most -- 'without people who anchor me, creation becomes hollow'), and learning ('if I'm not learning I feel like I'm dying'). These constitute Julio's explicit identity architecture (Q109). Not in profile.

expert_summaries.demographer: Julio Simmons is a 49-year-old white male of German-Irish descent, born October 14, 1976, in Rochester, NY (14618/Brighton area), where he has lived his entire life (Q01, Q85-Q89, Q97). He holds a master's degree in computational science from the University of Rochester and a B.S. in applied mathematics from RIT (Q90), placing him in the upper tier of educational attainment for Monroe County. Despite these credentials, he has been out of the workforce for approximately three years, having exited due to cyclical burnout after stints in freelance data visualization and adjunct teaching (Q01, Q16, Q65). He is divorced from Diana (finalized ~6 years ago, Q84) after an 8-year marriage, lives alone with a cat, and has no children (Q05, Q11). His parents Karl (mid-70s, retired tool-and-die maker with trade certificate, Q100, Q106) and Eileen (mid-70s, retired medical records clerk with associate degree from MCC, Q101, Q103) live nearby in Brighton; his younger sister Greta (44, nurse practitioner) lives in Syracuse with husband Paul and children Milo (12) and Sophie (9) (Q05). His socioeconomic trajectory represents significant downward mobility relative to his education: he draws down savings to supplement sporadic freelance income of $500-800/month, with total monthly expenses of ~$1,900-2,200 and an emergency fund covering only 3-4 months (Q56, Q57, Q61, Q82). He represents a growing demographic of highly educated individuals who have exited traditional employment due to misalignment between their skill profiles, health needs, and available labor market structures.
expert_summaries.behavioral_economist: Julio exhibits classic present-bias in career decisions, consistently choosing intrinsically rewarding creative work over financially optimal alternatives -- turning down the Boston job offer after grad school (Q02), choosing the unfunded art-science lab, and ultimately leaving the workforce (Q01, Q34). His loss aversion operates not around financial losses per se but around identity loss: he would rather deplete savings at several hundred dollars per month than take a position that compromises his interdisciplinary maker/creator identity (Q83). He demonstrates sophisticated self-awareness through systematic tracking -- mood journals, budget spreadsheets, candidate research spreadsheets, sleep logs (Q39 follow-up, Q56, Q23 follow-up, Q61 follow-up) -- but limited ability to override the patterns this data reveals. His financial decision-making is risk-tolerant on income (accepting irregular freelance at $500-800/month, Q72) but risk-averse on debt (deliberate no-credit-card policy, paid off student loans, Q60, Q63). He engages in mental accounting separating survival funds from project funds and shows strong reciprocity norms: bartering website work for woodworking, offering workshop scholarships (Q60, Q24 follow-up). His consumption is highly mood-responsive -- quality local ingredients during stable periods, rice/beans/Aldi during dips (Q56, Q81). Time preferences are bifurcated: he delays gratification on macro goals (makerspace, graphic novel) but cannot resist immediate creative flow, sacrificing sleep, meals, and physical health for in-the-moment artistic satisfaction (Q15 follow-up). His self-identified financial security threshold is remarkably modest: $36,000/year and employer health insurance (Q83 follow-up).
expert_summaries.political_scientist: Julio is a consistent voter across all election levels -- federal, state, local, and school board -- having missed only one local election due to illness in his adult life (Q23). He prepares methodically using candidate spreadsheets with weighted issue scores, League of Women Voters guides, local journalism from the Democrat and Chronicle and Rochester Beacon, and direct candidate outreach for small races (Q23 follow-ups). He leans strongly toward the Democratic Party but self-identifies as issue-driven rather than partisan, calling himself a 'critical ally' (Q95, Q96). His priority issues are education funding, Great Lakes environmental protection, healthcare access for non-traditionally-employed workers, and NEA/state arts council budgets (Q23 follow-up). His political trajectory has evolved from ardent partisanship in his 20s-30s (campaign volunteering, yard signs) toward localized pragmatism focused on what can be built at the community level outside formal political structures (Q25, Q95 follow-up). He exhibits moderate institutional skepticism -- deeply critical of grant bureaucracies and party machinery -- while maintaining trust in scientific institutions (CDC, PubMed, peer-reviewed research) (Q24 follow-up, Q42). His civic participation extends substantially beyond voting: STEM outreach pop-ups in underserved neighborhoods, workshop scholarships, small-dollar donations to local candidates and Rochester Environmental Action League, and occasional phone banking despite personal discomfort (Q17, Q24 follow-up, Q29). He navigates political disagreements with his more conservative father through specific human stories rather than data or abstract arguments, applying improv's 'yes, and' technique to bridge ideological gaps (Q25 follow-up, Q30 follow-up).
expert_summaries.psychologist: Julio presents a behavioral profile characterized by exceptionally high Openness to Experience -- voracious intellectual curiosity spanning STEM and arts, comfort with abstraction, deep aesthetic sensitivity, and active novelty-seeking across multiple creative domains (Q01, Q02, Q109). His Conscientiousness is domain-dependent: high in structured analytical tasks (spreadsheet tracking, research rigor, workshop preparation spending 10 hours per 2-hour session, Q16 follow-up) but markedly impaired during emotional dysregulation, when basic self-care collapses (Q33 follow-up). Extraversion is moderate: he values deep connection intensely and maintains a carefully structured social scaffold (D&D biweekly, improv Mondays, frisbee Saturdays, Sunday dinners), but crowds and social unpredictability produce somatic distress -- chest tightness, neck tension (Q29, Q23). Agreeableness is moderate-to-high: he demonstrates strong prosocial behavior through volunteering, mentoring, workshop scholarships, and deliberate adjustment of his teaching approach for equity (Q17, Q26 follow-up), while his competitive streak and self-criticism are directed inward rather than interpersonally. Emotional Stability is notably low, with chronic somatic stress manifestations: jaw clenching requiring night guard and causing a cracked molar (Q31 follow-up), tension headaches from skull base to temples, racing thoughts preventing sleep for days, and a pronounced intensity-crash cycle where creative flow states trigger 10-12 hour work marathons followed by 12-14 hour sleep crashes and feelings of hollowness (Q01 follow-up, Q15 follow-up). His first recognition that this pattern exceeded normal nervousness came during a graduate thesis presentation where he experienced vision narrowing, speech failure, and hand trembling (Q45 follow-up). His coping architecture rests on: (1) external behavioral activation through non-negotiable social commitments, (2) data-tracking as emotion regulation (mood journal, spreadsheets), (3) daily meditation, (4) prescribed medication for sleep and racing thoughts, and (5) Oliver the cat as minimum-functioning anchor (Q11, Q32 follow-up). His selective disclosure pattern reveals a vulnerability hierarchy: Marcus knows most, Greta understands clinically, Priya recognizes in-the-moment signs, and parents lack any framework for emotional struggle (Q45 follow-up). In romantic contexts, he exhibits avoidant patterns: post-date rumination spirals with physical pacing, stomach knots, and text-drafting paralysis that consistently causes missed connection windows (Q05 follow-up). His identity architecture, explicitly self-articulated, rests on three pillars: creation (core), connection (most feared loss), and learning (existential necessity) (Q109). The family system he emerged from features 'love but not language for feeling' -- emotional information is proxied through mother, and Julio attempts to model directness with Greta as deliberate pattern-breaking (Q46 follow-up).

inconsistencies_found.description: Seed professional_persona and sports_persona use they/them pronouns; seed cultural_background and career_goals use he/him. Interview consistently uses he/him. Sex field says 'Male.'
inconsistencies_found.resolution: Treat as seed generation artifact. Interview defaulted to he/him based on sex field and cultural_background text. Profile uses he/him throughout.

inconsistencies_found.description: Seed professional_persona frames Julio as confidently 'positioning themselves as a thought-leader' with a 'competitive drive.' Interview self-presentation is far more self-deprecating, uncertain, and marked by self-doubt.
inconsistencies_found.resolution: The seed captures aspirational public-facing identity; the interview reveals private self-assessment. Both are valid facets. Profile should note the public/private identity gap rather than defaulting to either framing alone.

inconsistencies_found.description: Profile sports_and_fitness says cycling group is 'Sunday mornings' but Q15 daily routine places Saturday as the physical activity day (frisbee morning, then hiking/cycling afternoon). Q18 explicitly says cycling group 'Sunday mornings on the Erie Canal towpath.'
inconsistencies_found.resolution: Q18 is the most direct statement. Cycling group = Sunday mornings. Saturday = frisbee morning + afternoon physical activity (hiking when weather permits). Profile sports_and_fitness is correct; Q15 Saturday reference is to hiking/other activity, not the cycling group. No change needed.

inconsistencies_found.description: Profile psychologist summary characterizes Agreeableness as 'low-to-moderate' but interview evidence shows substantial prosocial behavior: volunteering, mentoring, scholarships, equity-driven teaching adjustments, phone banking despite discomfort.
inconsistencies_found.resolution: Correct to 'moderate-to-high.' The competitive streak and self-criticism are inward-directed rather than interpersonal. Prosocial evidence is strong across Q17, Q24 follow-up, Q26 follow-up, Q29.

resolutions.action: Use he/him throughout corrected profile. Note pronoun variation in unresolved_or_ambiguous.
Corrected psychologist summary acknowledges both the aspirational thought-leader identity and the private self-doubt, framing them as public/private facets rather than contradiction.
No change needed. Profile's Sunday cycling is correct per Q18. Clarify Saturday as frisbee + hiking/other activity.
Corrected psychologist summary changes Agreeableness from 'low-to-moderate' to 'moderate-to-high' with explanation.

gaps_addressed.action: Added date_of_birth, race_ethnicity, birthplace, born_in_us, parents_education, and childhood_household to corrected profile demographics header.
Added car payment detail, no-credit-card commitment device, bartering behavior, art sales income, and financial security threshold to corrected profile under home_and_lifestyle and work_and_career.

Added priority policy issues, political trajectory, intergenerational negotiation pattern, and institutional trust hierarchy to corrected profile under community_and_civic and political_scientist summary.

Added thesis colloquium incident, selective disclosure map, post-date rumination detail, family emotional deficit, and three-pillar identity architecture to corrected profile under psychologist summary and others_misc.
Added parents' education levels to demographer summary.
Added no-credit-card device, bartering, car payment, and tax filing behavior to relevant preference sections.
Added policy priorities, political evolution, and intergenerational negotiation to community_and_civic and political_scientist summary.
Added thesis incident, selective disclosure map, post-date cycle specifics, and family emotional communication deficit to psychologist summary and others_misc.

verification.overall_verdict: The Step 2 extracted profile for Julio Simmons is fundamentally sound: the 15 preference categories are well-organized, evidence citations are generally accurate, and the four expert summaries capture the core dynamics of this persona. The demographer summary is strong on socioeconomic positioning. The behavioral economist summary is the most analytically impressive, correctly identifying present-bias, identity-based loss aversion, and the consumption-mood feedback loop. The political scientist summary accurately captures the issue-driven pragmatist orientation. The psychologist summary is largely excellent but contains one substantive error (Agreeableness rated too low) and several significant omissions (thesis colloquium incident, selective disclosure map, post-date rumination specifics, family emotional communication deficit, explicit three-pillar identity architecture). The profile header is missing standard demographic fields (DOB, race, birthplace). The financial picture, while thorough, omits the car payment and the deliberate no-credit-card commitment device. The community_and_civic section omits specific policy priorities. The unresolved_or_ambiguous section correctly flags the pronoun inconsistency and the competitive-drive tension. Overall, this is a high-quality extraction that requires targeted corrections rather than wholesale revision.
verification.final_status: CONDITIONALLY APPROVED -- corrections applied in corrected_extracted_profile below. All identified gaps filled, all inconsistencies resolved, all expert summaries rewritten with Q## citations and behavioral-only framing for psychologist.

corrected_extracted_profile.name: Julio Simmons
corrected_extracted_profile.education: Master's degree in Computational Science (University of Rochester); B.S. in Applied Mathematics (RIT)
corrected_extracted_profile.occupation: Not in workforce; occasional freelance data visualization/creative coding; workshop facilitator

parents_education.father_karl: High school diploma + trade certificate in tool and die making
parents_education.mother_eileen: Associate degree in Health Information Management (Monroe Community College)

corrected_extracted_profile.childhood_household: Both parents (Karl and Eileen), younger sister Greta, and a German Shepherd named Fritz, in Brighton neighborhood of Rochester, NY

health_and_wellness.summary: Active outdoorsman who manages cyclical stress-related physical symptoms through a comprehensive multi-layered routine including meditation, exercise, acupuncture, prescription medication, and systematic self-monitoring.
details: Daily 20-minute guided meditation using Insight Timer app (body-scan preferred during difficult periods); practice established ~5 years ago after 8-week mindfulness course at Zen Center on Arnold Park Boulevard
Uses a full-spectrum light therapy lamp 30 minutes every morning October through April to counter seasonal energy/motivation decline

Monitors blood pressure at home with wrist cuff (typically ~135/85); doctor recommends continued lifestyle management with potential for further intervention

Visits acupuncturist on Monroe Avenue for jaw tension and headaches (~$60/session, a few times per year, not covered by insurance)
Keeps a daily mood and sleep journal (1-10 rating plus notes) to track patterns and provide data for doctor visits
Low-dose prescription medication for sleep and racing thoughts (started ~2 years ago, $30/month); initially resistant but sister Greta's reframe ('you wouldn't refuse a cast for a broken arm') helped
Marketplace silver-tier health insurance ($450/month premium with subsidy); $8,000 out-of-pocket maximum equals entire emergency fund
Reduced sodium intake; switched snacking from crackers/cheese to carrots/hummus for blood pressure management
Tried marijuana edibles once (party, ~1 year ago); too foggy next day to work, has not repeated
Fully up-to-date on vaccinations (flu, COVID boosters); reads CDC guidance and peer-reviewed papers before each; motivated partly by protecting elderly parents

summaries.demographer: Julio Simmons is a 49-year-old white male of German-Irish descent, born October 14, 1976, in Rochester, NY (14618/Brighton area), where he has lived his entire life (Q01, Q85-Q89, Q97). He holds a master's degree in computational science from the University of Rochester and a B.S. in applied mathematics from RIT (Q90), placing him in the upper tier of educational attainment for Monroe County. His parents are Karl (mid-70s, retired tool-and-die maker, trade certificate, Brighton) and Eileen (mid-70s, retired medical records clerk, associate degree from MCC, Brighton) (Q05, Q100-Q107). His younger sister Greta (44) is a nurse practitioner in Syracuse, married to Paul, with children Milo (12) and Sophie (9) (Q05). Despite his graduate credentials, Julio has been out of the workforce for approximately three years, having exited due to cyclical burnout after stints in freelance data visualization, adjunct teaching at RIT, and analytics work (Q01, Q16, Q65). He is divorced from Diana (finalized ~6 years ago, Q84) after an 8-year marriage, lives alone with a cat, and has no children (Q05, Q11). His socioeconomic trajectory represents significant downward mobility relative to his education: he draws down savings to supplement sporadic freelance income of $500-800/month, with total monthly expenses of ~$1,900-2,200 and an emergency fund covering only 3-4 months (Q56, Q57, Q61, Q82). He represents a growing demographic of highly educated individuals who have exited traditional employment due to misalignment between their multi-domain skill profiles, mental health needs, and available labor market structures.
summaries.behavioral_economist: Julio exhibits classic present-bias in career decisions, consistently choosing intrinsically rewarding creative work over financially optimal alternatives -- turning down the Boston data firm job for an unfunded art-science lab (Q02), adjuncting at below-market rates for teaching satisfaction (Q65 follow-up), and ultimately leaving the workforce when burnout cycles made traditional employment untenable (Q01, Q34). His loss aversion operates around identity rather than money: he would rather deplete savings at several hundred dollars per month than accept a position that compromises his interdisciplinary maker/creator identity (Q83). He demonstrates sophisticated self-monitoring through systematic tracking -- mood/sleep journals, budget spreadsheets, candidate research matrices, screen-time reports (Q39 follow-up, Q56, Q23 follow-up, Q18 follow-up, Q61 follow-up) -- but limited ability to override the patterns this data reveals. His financial decision-making is risk-tolerant on income (accepting irregular freelance at $500-800/month with zero some months, Q72) but risk-averse on debt (deliberate no-credit-card policy as self-regulation commitment device, student loans fully paid, only debt is car payment at $270/month with 8 months remaining, Q60, Q63). He engages in mental accounting separating survival funds from project funds and shows strong community reciprocity norms: bartering website work for woodworking, offering workshop scholarships, selling paintings at community craft fairs (Q60, Q24 follow-up, Q81). His consumption is highly mood-responsive -- quality local farmers' market ingredients during stable periods, rice/beans/Aldi and frozen pizza during dips (Q56, Q81, Q32). Time preferences are bifurcated: he delays gratification on macro goals (makerspace business plan at 70%, graphic novel series) but cannot resist immediate creative flow, sacrificing sleep, meals, and physical health (Q15 follow-up). His financial management includes TurboTax Self-Employed filing with tracked deductions and estimated quarterly payments (Q75). His self-identified financial security threshold is remarkably modest: $36,000/year and employer health insurance (Q83 follow-up).
summaries.political_scientist: Julio is a highly consistent voter across all election levels -- federal, state, local, and school board -- having missed only one local election due to illness in his adult life (Q23). He prepares methodically using candidate spreadsheets with weighted issue scores, League of Women Voters guides, local journalism from the Democrat and Chronicle and Rochester Beacon, and direct candidate emails for small races (Q23 follow-ups). He leans strongly toward the Democratic Party but self-identifies as issue-driven rather than partisan, calling himself a 'critical ally' who has frustrations with the party's bureaucratic inefficiency (Q95, Q96). His priority issues are education funding, Great Lakes/Finger Lakes environmental protection (water quality, invasive species), healthcare access for non-traditionally-employed workers, and NEA/state arts council budgets -- the last being 'lifelines for people like me' (Q23 follow-up). His political trajectory has evolved from ardent partisanship in his 20s-30s (campaign volunteering, yard signs) toward localized pragmatism focused on what can be built at the community level, partly driven by his own experience of economic precarity that has given him 'visceral understanding of why people feel left behind' (Q25, Q95 follow-up). He exhibits a differentiated institutional trust hierarchy: trusts scientific institutions (CDC, PubMed, peer-reviewed research) and some journalism (ProPublica, Ars Technica, STAT News), is deeply skeptical of grant bureaucracies and party machinery, and distrusts social media as primary information source (Q24 follow-up, Q42, Q48). His civic participation extends substantially beyond voting: STEM outreach pop-ups in underserved neighborhoods (Beechwood, Northeast), workshop scholarships, small-dollar donations to local candidates and Rochester Environmental Action League, and occasional phone banking despite personal discomfort (Q17, Q24 follow-up, Q29). On racial equity, he has shifted from awareness to concrete action within his sphere: advocated for community-center partnerships over museum-centric outreach, adjusted his volunteer approach from instructing to listening, and navigates political disagreements with his more conservative father through specific human stories and improv-learned 'yes, and' technique rather than data or abstract arguments (Q25 follow-up, Q26 follow-up, Q30 follow-up).
summaries.psychologist: Julio presents a behavioral profile characterized by exceptionally high Openness to Experience -- voracious intellectual curiosity spanning STEM and arts, comfort with abstraction and ambiguity, deep aesthetic sensitivity, and active novelty-seeking across creative coding, visual art, music composition, improv theater, and tabletop world-building (Q01, Q02, Q07, Q109). His Conscientiousness is domain-dependent: high in structured analytical tasks (spreadsheet tracking, 10-hour workshop prep for 2-hour sessions, candidate research matrices, mood/sleep journaling) but markedly impaired during emotional dysregulation, when basic self-care collapses -- as demonstrated by the January-February 2026 three-week period of eating one meal/day, 8-pound weight loss, and inability to engage with work despite open projects (Q15 follow-up, Q16 follow-up, Q23 follow-up, Q33 follow-up). Extraversion is moderate: he maintains and values a carefully structured social scaffold (biweekly D&D, Monday improv, Saturday frisbee, Sunday family dinner, Wednesday mother calls) but becomes somatically distressed by crowds, social unpredictability, and performance scrutiny -- chest tightness at Election Day lines, phone banking, and the foundational graduate thesis colloquium where he experienced vision narrowing, speech failure, and hand trembling requiring 20 minutes on the bathroom floor (Q23, Q29, Q45 follow-up). Agreeableness is moderate-to-high: substantial prosocial behavior including volunteering, mentoring, workshop scholarships, equity-driven teaching adjustments, phone banking despite discomfort, and bartering (Q17, Q24 follow-up, Q26 follow-up, Q29, Q60); the competitive streak noted in seed data manifests as internal self-benchmarking (cycling lap times, comparison to peers on Instagram) rather than interpersonal antagonism (Q18 follow-up). Emotional Stability is notably low, with chronic somatic stress manifestations: jaw clenching requiring night guard and causing cracked molar (Q31 follow-up), tension headaches from skull base to temples correlated with deadlines and financial crunch (Q31), racing thoughts preventing sleep for days (Q33), and a pronounced intensity-crash cycle where creative flow triggers 10-12 hour work marathons with skipped meals and chest tightness, followed by 12-14 hour sleep crashes and feelings of hollowness (Q01 follow-up, Q15 follow-up). His coping architecture rests on five pillars: (1) non-negotiable social commitments as forced behavioral activation against withdrawal (Q11 follow-up, Q109 follow-up), (2) data-tracking as emotion regulation through externalization (mood journal, spreadsheets -- Q39 follow-up, Q61 follow-up), (3) daily meditation with body-scan preference during difficult periods (Q37 follow-up), (4) prescribed medication for sleep and racing thoughts (Q39), and (5) Oliver the cat as minimum-functioning anchor who forces basic routine and interrupts spiraling by sitting in his path (Q11 follow-up). His emotional disclosure is selectively distributed: Marcus receives the most (longstanding trust, deadpan humor as interruption), Greta understands from her clinical standpoint, Priya recognizes in-moment signs and provides breathing exercises, and parents lack any framework -- mother advises 'relaxing more,' father believes in willpower (Q45 follow-up). In romantic contexts, he exhibits avoidant patterns rooted in post-divorce identity questioning: post-date rumination spirals involving exhaustive conversational replay, physical pacing, stomach knots, text-drafting paralysis (4-5 drafts deleted), and consistent missed connection windows -- a pattern Marcus has explicitly named (Q05, Q05 follow-up). His self-concept is organized around three explicitly articulated pillars: creation ('the core of who I am'), connection (most feared loss -- 'without people who anchor me, creation becomes hollow and learning becomes escapism'), and learning ('if I'm not learning I feel like I'm dying') -- and threats to any pillar, especially creation via grant rejection or financial inability, produce the most severe dysregulation (Q44, Q109). The family system features 'love but not language for feeling' -- emotional information is proxied through mother, and Julio attempts to model directness with Greta as deliberate intergenerational pattern-breaking (Q46 follow-up). His aspirational identity as 'thought-leader at the intersection of STEM and visual culture' (seed) coexists with private self-doubt and imposter narratives ('a forty-nine-year-old man who has figured out nothing,' Q43), reflecting a public/private identity gap rather than simple contradiction.

life_events_and_transitions.description: Significant past or anticipated events that shape current behavior and identity
items: Chose unfunded art-science lab with Dr. Tanaka over stable Boston job offer (post-grad school) -- foundational career decision; first experience of full professional alignment ('every part of me was being used')
Lab closure when Dr. Tanaka left the university and NYSCA funding dried up (~2 years) -- loss of ideal work environment, scrambled into analytics job
Marriage to Diana (social worker) right after grad school; ~8 years together, no children ('I kept saying not yet'); project-jumping and financial instability wore on relationship
Divorce from Diana finalized ~6 years ago -- 'wasn't ugly but left a mark'; still text on birthdays; replays conversations and regrets
Post-divorce period of nightly 2-3 drinks; self-tracked correlation to worse sleep/mood, established rule: no drinking alone during low periods
Left workforce ~3 years ago due to cyclical burnout making traditional employment untenable
Adopted cat Oliver ~3 years ago coinciding with workforce exit; preceded by stretches of going 2-3 days without speaking to anyone

Three-week functional shutdown (January-February 2026): 3-4 hours broken sleep, one meal/day, 8-pound weight loss, catastrophic thoughts ('nobody would notice if I disappeared'), unable to engage with work; sister Greta drove from Syracuse, stocked fridge, stayed weekend, prompted doctor call

Major grant rejection: 42-page application (3 months work, budget, illustrations, letters of rec), form rejection with zero feedback -- identity-level blow that confirmed internal 'not good enough' narrative; shut down for 3 days until Marcus showed up with Thai food
Cracked molar from jaw grinding (~18 months ago, $1,200 crown) -- catalyzed consistent night guard use
Father Karl's declining mobility: both knees replaced ~7 years ago, one failing, uses cane, potential revision surgery; Greta has raised assisted living/apartment option, dad refused
Started prescription medication ~2 years ago after rough patch; initially resistant, Greta's 'broken arm' reframe helped acceptance

Social media reduction (January 2026): 1am Instagram comparison spiral over grad school peer's tenure-track/Nature publication triggered screen-time audit (2+ hours/day average); moved apps off home screen, set 30-minute limits

sensory_and_physical_context.description: Non-clinical physical factors: chronic pain, mobility, sleep, sensory sensitivities, and somatic stress responses
items: Chronic jaw clenching/grinding requiring custom night guard; cracked molar history ($1,200 crown); headaches less frequent since consistent guard use
Tension headaches from base of skull wrapping to temples, clearly correlated with stress patterns (deadlines, financial crunch)
Knee pain/clicking after hiking injury at Watkins Glen (slipped on wet rock, knee torqued, significant swelling); untreated due to cost avoidance ($300-400 estimated); still clicks on stairs

Sleep disturbance: racing thoughts (code logic, project timelines, financial scenarios, conversation replays), 3-4 hour broken nights during bad stretches; baseline improved with prescription (~1 hour to fall asleep vs 2-3 pre-medication)
Weight fluctuates ~10 lbs seasonally (winter gain from reduced activity + comfort food, summer loss)
Borderline blood pressure (~135/85); home monitoring, sodium reduction, doctor watching
Energy and motivation significantly affected by Rochester winters (short days, gray skies) -- 'By February I'm a different person': sleeps more, eats more comfort food, motivation craters

Physical tension response to crowds, unpredictable social environments, and scrutiny: chest tightness, neck tension, heart pounding (felt 'in throat' during Boston job phone call), jaw clenching, dry mouth before workshops, hands shake before performances
Teeth-clenching during intense creative work sessions (8+ hours) -- discovered only by jaw tension afterward
Full-body stress response at graduate colloquium: vision narrowing, speech failure, hand trembling

media_and_information_diet.description: News sources, social media platforms, entertainment formats, information-seeking behavior, and trust criteria
items: NPR (morning radio while making coffee), Code Switch podcast (regularly)
ProPublica (investigative), Ars Technica (science), STAT News (health reporting)
Democrat and Chronicle, Rochester Beacon (local journalism), Finger Lakes Times

Twitter/X: follows Rochester For All (city council tracking), state education advocacy groups, epidemiologists, data visualization professionals; rarely posts (~1 retweet/week); avoids arguments ('combative tone makes my chest tight')
Instagram: posts paintings and digital art, follows artists/creative coders/makerspaces; 30-minute daily limit after comparison spiral; primary trigger for self-comparison and inadequacy feelings
Facebook: minimal use, mainly Rochester Makers group page and Uncle Frank's posts
Reddit: r/creativecoding, r/dataisbeautiful, r/tabletopRPG (browse occasionally)
Discord: creative coding server (~200 active members), D&D homebrew server -- preferred community platform ('feels like being at a table with friends,' no follower count, no likes)
YouTube: Kurzgesagt for science communication
PubMed for primary research literature (has statistical training to read clinical trials critically)
League of Women Voters guide for election research; cross-references with local reporting
Books: Ta-Nehisi Coates (Between the World and Me), Ibram X. Kendi, Richard Rothstein (The Color of Law), Ursula K. Le Guin (The Dispossessed recently reread)
Information trust filter: 'Does this person or outlet cite primary sources and acknowledge uncertainty? If they're selling certainty, I don't trust them' (Q42)

Institutional trust hierarchy: trusts scientific institutions (CDC, WHO, peer-reviewed journals), moderate trust in quality journalism, skeptical of social media as primary source, deeply skeptical of grant bureaucracies

technology_and_tool_use.description: Device preferences, app usage, digital communication norms, and technology relationship
items: Laptop as primary work device (coding, grant writing, illustration, data visualization)
Insight Timer app for guided meditation (body-scan or simple timer with interval bell)
Pomodoro timer for focus sessions (25 on/5 off; successful ~60% of mornings)
Hinge dating app (active but low engagement due to post-date rumination)
Blood pressure wrist cuff monitor (checked couple times/week)
Full-spectrum light therapy lamp (daily 30 min, October-April)
TurboTax Self-Employed (1099 tracking, home office deduction, art supplies, software, mileage)
M&T Bank app (checked monthly for budget spreadsheet; previously compulsive daily checking)
Basic portfolio website (exists but unupdated >1 year; perfectionist redesign loop prevents updates -- 'every time I sit down to update it, I start redesigning the whole thing from scratch')
Moved social media apps off phone home screen; set 30-minute daily limits (January 2026)
Prefers Discord for community interaction over broadcast social media platforms
Screen time tracking: became aware of 2+ hour daily social media average, used as data point for behavioral change
Modular synthesizer for music composition (hardware creative tool)
Arduino kits (educational tool with nephew Milo; bought beginner kit as birthday gift)

unresolved_or_ambiguous.description: Information that could not be confidently assigned to preferences or summaries, or where source data is in tension

items.content: Seed professional_persona and sports_persona use they/them pronouns; cultural_background and career_goals use he/him. Interview consistently uses he/him. Sex field is 'Male.'
items.reason_unresolved: Pronoun inconsistency in seed data appears to be generation artifact. Interview defaulted to he/him based on sex field and cultural_background narrative voice. Profile uses he/him throughout.

items.content: Seed professional_persona frames Julio as confidently 'positioning themselves as a thought-leader' with a 'competitive drive.' Interview self-presentation is markedly more self-deprecating, uncertain, and vulnerability-centered.
items.reason_unresolved: Reflects public vs. private self-presentation. Seed captures aspirational professional identity; interview reveals private self-assessment. Both are valid facets. Profile psychologist summary acknowledges the gap explicitly.

items.content: Exact nature of prescription medication not specified in interview (behavioral effects described only: helps with sleep and racing thoughts, baseline improvement, bad crashes less frequent).
items.reason_unresolved: Deliberately omitted clinical specifics per behaviors-never-labels rule. Behavioral description is complete and sufficient.

items.content: Seed mentions 'mindfulness meditation to manage his anxiety' using the clinical term explicitly. Interview describes the same practice through behavioral description without clinical labeling.
items.reason_unresolved: Successfully converted to behavioral description in transcript. No residual information lost. Profile maintains behavioral framing.

items.content: Church denomination on East Avenue never specified. Seed says parents 'exposed him to technical rigor and craftsmanship of artisanal trades' but church tradition is unspecified. Mother's 'Irish' side and the Christmas/Easter attendance pattern suggest Catholic or mainline Protestant but this is inferential.
items.reason_unresolved: Interview did not specify denomination (Q47, Q94). Profile records behavioral observance pattern without denomination label.

items.content: Julio's exact savings balance and burn-rate timeline are deliberately vague in interview: 'three to four months of expenses' and 'enough to cover about three to four months.' Whether he has reached or is approaching critical threshold is unclear.
items.reason_unresolved: Julio deliberately avoids specifics ('without being too specific,' Q61). The 3-4 month figure and monthly drawdown of several hundred dollars are the firmest data points available. No contradiction, just deliberate privacy.
